title: Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition
subtitle: The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.
caption:
Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition

The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.

This choropleth map displays the estimated rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition per 100,000 children under the age of 5 for the year 2023. The data is visualized using a color scale ranging from light beige for the lowest rates to dark maroon for the highest, with specific thresholds marked at 0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1,000, and 2,000 deaths per 100,000. The highest concentrations of mortality, represented by the darkest red shades indicating rates between 500 and over 2,000, are clustered primarily across Sub-Saharan Africa. Countries such as Somalia, Chad, the Central African Republic, and Nigeria fall within these upper bands, alongside Afghanistan and Yemen in Asia. A secondary band of elevated rates, generally falling between 100 and 500 deaths per 100,000, covers a broad swath of territory including India, Pakistan, and much of the remaining African continent south of the Sahara. In contrast, North America, Europe, Australia, and the southern cone of South America are shaded in the lightest beige tones, corresponding to rates near or below 10 deaths per 100,000. Greenland is marked with a hatched pattern, signifying that no data is available for that region. The visual distribution highlights a stark disparity, with the most severe rates concentrated in specific regions of Africa and South Asia, while the majority of the Americas, Europe, and Oceania show significantly lower figures.
